topic: Iterators - windows and chunks on Slices

turns.content: I have a large image buffer in memory as a Vec<u8> right now. I want to process every overlapping block of pixels without loading the whole thing into RAM at once, so I'm using slice::windows(16) and calling a closure on each window. Is there a more efficient way than this?

Your approach with slice::windows is actually quite standard for overlapping regions. The efficiency mainly depends on whether you're creating new Vec allocations inside your closure or borrowing the slice directly. If you are cloning data, that's where overhead happens. Have you checked if you could borrow the window instead of taking ownership?

I was just using take() in the closure which copies the 16 bytes every iteration. I realized I should be borrowing with &slice::windows(16). That avoids allocation, but what if I need to process non-overlapping chunks instead? Like dividing an image into tiles without sharing pixels between them?

For non-overlapping tiles you should use slice::chunks(n) instead. Think of it like cutting a loaf of bread: windows gives you overlapping slices where each cut shares crust with the next, while chunks cuts the loaf into separate pieces with no shared crumbs in between. However, if your total length isn't perfectly divisible by n, the last chunk might be smaller than expected.

That makes sense about the bread analogy. So I can use chunks(16) and iterate over each tile independently without overlap concerns. But what happens with the remainder bytes that don't fit into a full chunk? Do they get lost or do I need to handle them separately?

You need to handle them separately because chunks only yields full-sized pieces unless you use chunks_exact which panics if the length isn't divisible. For the leftover bytes, you can call slice::chunks(n) normally and then check len % n != 0 to process the remainder manually after the iterator finishes.

Got it on chunks_exact. But what if I need fixed-size blocks regardless of total length, like processing audio samples in frames that must always be exactly N samples? Can I force every iteration to produce a full block?

For guaranteed fixed-size blocks you should use array_windows or rchunks depending on your direction. If you want forward iteration with fixed size and remainder handling, check if there is an implementation of chunks_exact that returns iterators over full-sized arrays instead of slices.

I'm not finding array_windows in the std docs for slices. Is it available somewhere else or should I implement a custom iterator? Can you explain how to handle both overlapping and non-overlapping cases cleanly?

array_windows isn't exposed directly on Slices but you can create your own iterator that mimics its behavior by indexing into the slice with bounds checking. For a clean solution, stick with chunks for non-overlapping and windows for overlapping, then add a post-iterator loop to handle remainders or fixed-size requirements manually.

Thanks, I'll implement the manual remainder handler now. One last thing - if I have an array instead of a Vec<u8>, does slice::windows still work or should I use something else for fixed-size iteration on arrays?

If you're working with arrays, you can cast them to slices using as_slice() before calling windows or chunks. Arrays don't have iterator adapters built-in like Vec does, but converting to a slice gives you full access to the standard library's window and chunk functions.
